text: In this video, we demonstrate robotic assisted low anterior resection beyond TME approach for locally advanced CA rectum.
Fifty-year-old postmenopausal lady, ECOG-I, no comorbidity, BMI of twenty-three point one, evaluated for bleeding PR.
Clinical examination was unremarkable.
The colonoscopy reveals a circumferential lesion located eight centimeters from the anal verge, visible within the rectal lumen.
Biopsy moderately differentiated adeno CA.
CEA was seventeen
MRI showed asymmetrical mass involving the upper rectum with multiple pre-rectal lymph nodes.
There was a pre-sacral lymph node involving the pre-sacral fascia, CRM was positive, EMVI was positive and the anterior peritoneal reflection was involved by the disease.
The patient received short course RT, twenty-five gray, five gray in five fractions, followed by four cycles of chemotherapy, Folfox.
Reassessment MRI showed interval decrease in the size of the lesion.
The disease straddled anterior peritoneal reflection.
CRM was positive at twelve o'clock and there was extra-emisorectral disease on the left side.
The surgeon performs a robotic-assisted low anterior resection using a left vascular approach, excising the pre-sacral fascia.
The surgeon performs a staple anastomosis, followed by the creation of a divergent loop ileostomy, completing the surgical procedure.
The Da Vinci XI robotic system is utilized with standard robotic ports placed in the left lower abdomen.
The surgical team uses the R-II camera port with a 30-degree telescope to provide a visual perspective of the operative field.
The surgeon begins the dissection by incising the medial peritoneal fold near the inferior mesenteric vein, using precise cuts to separate the tissue.
The surgeon develops a dissection plane beneath the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), maintaining proximity to the vein during the procedure.
A vascular plane is created beneath the inferior mesenteric vein using robotic assistance, with the camera providing a view through a thirty-degree telescope.
The surgeon continues dissection close to the mesocolic fascia, progressing in a medial to lateral direction using the Da Vinci XI robotic system.
The surgeon dissects the inferior mesenteric vein up to the duodenojejunal flexure, using a thirty-degree telescope through the R-II camera port for visualization.
The surgeon performs central venous ligation and applies a high clip to the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), securing the vessel within the dissected plane.
The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, exposing the pancreas superiorly and creating an entry into the lesser sac.
The surgeon lifts the sigmoid colon to make the groove more prominent, then cuts the peritoneum over the groove, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon continues dissection close to the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), carefully sparing the surrounding nerves.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter and carefully drops it away from the surgical field, ensuring it is not involved in the ongoing dissection.
The surgeon continues dissection towards the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), carefully preserving and dropping down all visible nerves in the surgical field.
The surgeon skeletonizes the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) route, revealing the artery and surrounding structures while preserving the nearby nerves.

The surgeon performs high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) with clips at its origin. Medial to lateral dissection is carried out in the avascular plane towards the lateral abdominal wall. The lateral peritoneal attachment is incised to mobilize the descending and sigmoid colon.
The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure by cutting the lateral peritoneal attachment, allowing for further movement of the colon.
The surgeon fully mobilizes the splenic flexure, providing additional length to facilitate the upcoming colorectal anastomosis.
The surgeon continues the dissection posteriorly, working close to the mesorectal fascia, carefully separating the tissue layers in this region.
The surgeon continues posterior dissection near the mesorectal fascia, carefully preserving visible nerves in the surgical field.
The right hypogastric nerve is clearly visible in the surgical field, with surrounding tissues carefully dissected to preserve the nerve's integrity.
The surgeon continues the posterior dissection near the mesorectal fascia, preserving visible nerves in the surgical field.
The surgeon changes the plane of dissection and excises the presacral fascia, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The presacral venous flexure is clearly visible during the posterior dissection near the mesorectal fascia.
Atmosphere has to be taken in order to avoid the injury and bleed.
The surgeon adopts a vascular approach on the left side, addressing the extra major rectal disease, with visible manipulation of surrounding vascular structures.
The surgeon cuts the anterior leaf of the broad ligament and extends the peritoneal incision along the external iliac vessels, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon exposes the iliac vessels and resects the obliterated umbilical artery, revealing the anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully resects the left ureter and lateralizes it away from the resection site, ensuring it is positioned safely to avoid damage during the procedure.
The surgeon continues dissection along the left internal iliac vessel, carefully navigating around the vascular structures.
The surgeon sacrifices the nerves on the left side and removes the entire tissue from the lateral pelvic wall, including the pre-sacral fascia, as part of the specimen.
The surgeon cuts the anterior peritoneal fold and develops a plane between the rectum and the vagina, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon performs right lateral pelvic mobilization of the rectum, carefully preserving the surrounding nerve structures during the procedure.
The surgeon continues anterior dissection, progressing along the plane between the rectum and adjacent structures.
The surgeon cuts the rectovaginal septum anteriorly, allowing for further mobilization of the rectal tube within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon completes the circumferential mobilization of the rectum, ensuring it is fully freed from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides the rectal specimen using an endo stapler, completing the separation of the tissue.
The specimen is delivered through the ileostomy site, completing the extraction process.
The surgeon performs an intracorporeal stapled end-to-end colorectal anastomosis using a circular stapler, joining the ends of the colon within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon creates a diversion loop ileostomy, visually forming a loop of the ileum that is brought to the surface of the abdomen.
Postoperative period was uneventful and the patient was discharged on post of day seven.
Final histopathological report revealed pathological complete response
and the patient was planned for adjuvant chemotherapy.
